topic: Should universities ban AI in coursework?

seeds.name: @edith_examboard
seeds.bio: A 58-year-old classics professor who chairs her faculty's academic standards committee.
seeds.stance: Universities should ban AI in assessed coursework until they can prove authorship and protect academic standards.

seeds.name: @jay_builds
seeds.bio: A 22-year-old computer science finalist who tutors first-years and ships small AI tools on weekends.
seeds.stance: Banning AI teaches students to hide useful tools instead of learning how to use them well.

seeds.name: @maya_kpi
seeds.bio: A 34-year-old learning designer helping lecturers rewrite assessments after the first wave of AI panic.
seeds.stance: Universities should allow AI with disclosure, oral checks, and coursework that values process over polished output.

seeds.name: @sam_methods
seeds.bio: A 41-year-old education researcher who studies assessment validity and dislikes policy by anecdote.
seeds.stance: Universities should test which AI policies improve learning before choosing bans or blanket permission.

seeds.name: @nina_deadline
seeds.bio: A 20-year-old politics undergraduate balancing coursework, shifts at a cafe, and a student radio slot.
seeds.stance: The AI policy matters less than whether assignments are clear, fairly marked, and realistic for students.
